region_of_origin: Central Asia (widely cultivated across Southeast Asia and globally)

serving.natural_language_portion: One medium raw shallot (approximately 30 g) is a typical serving used as a flavour base in cooking or consumed raw in salads and condiments.

nutrient_source.title: Shallots, raw — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for raw shallot (cr_id F14040056). All core macros and secondary nutrients are exact matches from this approved primary source. Fatty acid breakdown and selected micronutrients supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 11677) where HPB does not report those fields.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal); University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value for raw shallot is listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 15 is estimated by analogy with onion (GI ~10–15, University of Sydney GI Database) and spring onion, both Allium genus vegetables with similar carbohydrate composition and fibre content. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (15 × 5.04) / 100 ≈ 0.8 for a 30 g serving (5.04 g carbohydrate).

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (12 mg/100 g) and high potassium (334 mg/100 g) providing a favourable Na:K ratio supportive of blood pressure regulation
hypertension.guidance: Raw shallots are suitable for individuals with hypertension; their high potassium-to-sodium ratio supports the DASH dietary pattern, and quercetin content has demonstrated vasodilatory effects in clinical studies — avoid fried shallot preparations that add sodium and saturated fat.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~15), 3.2 g dietary fibre per 100 g, and bioactive quercetin and allicin compounds with evidence-based hypoglycaemic properties
type2_diabetes.guidance: Raw shallots are appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; the low GI, moderate fibre content, and naturally occurring sugars in whole-food matrix result in minimal postprandial glucose excursion — consume as a flavour base or condiment rather than in large isolated quantities.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible fat (0.1 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, high potassium, and quercetin flavonoid content with anti-platelet and LDL-oxidation-inhibiting properties
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Raw shallots are cardioprotective in the context of a balanced diet; their quercetin and organosulfur compounds (allicin) have been associated with reduced LDL oxidation and platelet aggregation in peer-reviewed studies — avoid deep-fried shallot preparations that introduce saturated fat and excess calories.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Rich in quercetin (anti-inflammatory flavonoid), allicin, and dietary fibre supporting gut microbiome health relevant to immune regulation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Raw shallots are suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to their anti-inflammatory phytochemical profile; patients on NSAIDs who experience GI sensitivity may prefer lightly cooked shallots to reduce digestive discomfort.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Raw shallot (Allium cepa var. aggregatum) is a small, elongated bulb vegetable with a distinctive purple-pink outer skin and pale cream-to-purple interior flesh. It is consumed with the outer papery skin removed and the root end trimmed. Raw shallots have a firm, crisp texture and a flavour profile that is milder and sweeter than common onions, with subtle garlic undertones derived from organosulfur compounds including allicin. They are widely used across Southeast Asian, South Asian, European, and Middle Eastern cuisines as a raw condiment, flavour base, or garnish. In Singapore and Malaysia, raw shallots are a foundational ingredient in sambal, rempah spice pastes, and salad dressings. Nutritionally, raw shallots provide 72 kcal per 100 g with 16.8 g carbohydrate, 2.5 g protein, and only 0.1 g fat. Dietary fibre at 3.2 g/100 g supports glycaemic moderation and gut health. Sodium is very low at 12 mg/100 g while potassium is high at 334 mg/100 g, making shallots particularly suitable for hypertension management under DASH dietary principles. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 15 (low), consistent with other Allium genus vegetables. Shallots are rich in quercetin, a flavonoid with anti-inflammatory, anti-platelet, and vasodilatory properties supported by peer-reviewed clinical evidence. They are naturally vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher.
common_user_queries: Can I eat shallots if I have high blood pressure?
What is the difference between shallot and onion?
Is shallot suitable for a low-sodium diet?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F14040056), an approved primary source with confidence 0.95. GI is estimated by analogy with onion from the University of Sydney GI Database, which is the approved GI source; this introduces a minor uncertainty but the analogous estimate is well-supported by Allium genus literature. Ontology codes are defensible from FoodOn, FoodEx2, and SNOMED CT.
notes: Saturated_fat_g (0.02), monounsaturated_fat_g (0.01), and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.04) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for raw shallots (NDB 11677) as HPB does not report fatty acid breakdown; these values are consistent with the total fat of 0.1 g/100 g reported by HPB. Trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed negligible for raw plant foods per USDA SR Legacy. Cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero for all plant-derived foods; confirmed zero per HPB and USDA FoodData Central. Vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as shallots are a plant food with no B12. Vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — negligible in raw allium vegetables per USDA SR Legacy. Vitamin_c_mg (8.0), B-vitamins, folate_mcg_dfe, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 11677) where HPB does not report these fields. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is set to -1: RAE-expressed vitamin A data for shallots is not consistently reported in either the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 11677) in a form suitable for direct citation without risk of misattribution; this field is therefore marked as data not available. vitamin_e_mg is set to -1: tocopherol data for shallots is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database and is absent from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 11677) for this food entry; this field is therefore marked as data not available. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on onion GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct shallot GI entry exists in the database as of April 2026. Glycemic load per serving = (15 × 5.04 g carbohydrate in 30 g serving) / 100 = 0.76, rounded to 0.8.
